Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Ron Chrisley

contemporary

Synthetic phenomenology: consciousness engineering as a philosophical method.

The best way to read Ron Chrisley is not as offering one isolated “theory of consciousness,” but as developing a research program at the intersection of philosophy of mind, AI, cognitive science, robotics, and machine consciousness. The reconstructed view is a form of constructive, embodied, virtual-machine functionalism, combined with a revisionary but non-eliminativist account of qualia and a distinctive method Chrisley calls interactive empiricism. In that view, consciousness is not a mysterious non-physical substance, not merely behavior, not merely scale in AI, and not necessarily limited to biological brains. It is an architecture-sensitive cluster of phenomena involving embodied content, sensorimotor and metacognitive capacities, internal self-observation, and concept-use practices that may themselves have to be transformed through engineering. Chrisley’s position is therefore historically important because it mediates between classical computationalism, embodied/enactive cognition, Dennett-style heterophenomenology, Chalmers-style hard-problem pressure, and contemporary artificial-consciousness research.

Chrisley’s own career sits exactly at that boundary. A 2025 academic-event bio describes Chrisley as Professor of Cognitive Science and Artificial Intelligence at the University of Sussex, Director of the Sussex Centre for Cognitive Science, and a researcher with roughly four decades of work across AI, cognitive science, and philosophy. That same source summarizes Chrisley’s current public stance on AI consciousness as rejecting two extremes: that AI consciousness is impossible in principle, and that current or merely scaled-up AI already has it.

What follows is a reconstruction of the philosophy of mind implicit in Chrisley’s writings. I distinguish what is central and well supported in the corpus from what remains programmatic or open.

1. Historical location: after classical functionalism, before current AI-consciousness panic

Chrisley’s work is best placed in the late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century transition from classical computational philosophy of mind to embodied, situated, and artificial-consciousness research. Classical functionalism, broadly, holds that a mental state is individuated by its role in a system rather than by its intrinsic material constitution; it became a major philosophical position in the last third of the twentieth century. The computational theory of mind similarly treated thought and cognition as computational processes. Chrisley inherits that tradition, but does not accept the narrow version in which computation means disembodied symbol manipulation or a fixed Turing-machine-style formalism.

The other crucial historical pressure is the reaction against “Good Old-Fashioned AI” and against simple symbolic theories of mind. Embodied cognition became a broad interdisciplinary program spanning philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, robotics, AI, and related fields, emphasizing the dependence of cognition on bodily capacities and environmental embedding. Chrisley’s 1995 “Taking Embodiment Seriously” belongs to this movement, but with an important twist: embodiment is not used to reject computation; rather, it is used to revise what counts as an adequate computational or representational account.

A third historical background is the philosophy of consciousness after the “hard problem,” philosophical zombies, and the Knowledge Argument. The hard problem asks why physical or functional processes are accompanied by experience at all; zombie arguments ask whether beings physically identical to us could lack experience; the Knowledge Argument asks whether Mary, who supposedly knows all physical facts about color but has never seen red, learns something non-physical when she first sees red. Chrisley does not simply deny that these arguments are serious. Instead, Chrisley repeatedly tries to show that the concepts generating these problems can be transformed by architecture, artificial systems, metacognition, and improved ways of specifying experiential content.

The fourth background is Searle’s Chinese Room argument and the broader debate over whether artificial systems could genuinely understand or be conscious. Searle’s argument was directed against strong AI, not against the weaker claim that computers can be useful tools for psychological explanation. Chrisley’s work is historically situated after that argument: Chrisley repeatedly resists the inference from “standard symbolic computation is inadequate” to “artificial consciousness is impossible.”

Finally, Chrisley’s work now connects to the contemporary debate about whether current AI systems could be conscious. A 2023 scientific report on AI consciousness concluded that no current AI systems were conscious, while also arguing that there are no obvious technical barriers to systems satisfying candidate consciousness indicators. Chrisley’s current public position is strikingly similar in spirit: current AI models should not be treated as conscious, but there is no principled reason to rule out non-biological conscious AI.

2. The governing thesis: philosophy of mind must become constructive

The most distinctive methodological idea in Chrisley’s work is interactive empiricism. Chrisley’s claim is not merely that empirical science helps philosophy. It is stronger: certain philosophical breakthroughs about consciousness may require philosophers to interact with engineered systems, and perhaps even to help build them. This challenges the traditional separation between conceptual analysis and experimental or engineering work.

The reason is that our current concept of consciousness may be inadequate. Philosophers often assume that if the right argument is written clearly enough, consciousness will become conceptually tractable. Chrisley doubts this. On this view, some conceptual capacities are skill-like: they are not acquired solely by reading propositions, but by learning to navigate relations between one’s experiential perspective and objective, physical, or computational descriptions. Consciousness is especially difficult because it demands that we move between first-person experiential presentation and third-person scientific explanation.

Interactive empiricism therefore says that concept formation can require interactive experience with the subject matter. In the case of artificial consciousness, that means that design, construction, use, and modification of artificial systems are not merely downstream applications of a finished philosophy. They can be part of the philosophical method itself. The philosopher-engineer interaction becomes a developmental loop: build a system, discover the limits of current concepts, revise the concepts, build a better system, and repeat.

This is why Chrisley’s philosophy of mind is not armchair functionalism. It is a constructive philosophy of mind. It treats artificial systems as epistemic instruments: not only possible bearers of mental states, but also tools for clarifying what our mental-state concepts mean. In this sense, artificial consciousness is not a side issue for Chrisley. It is one of the central ways philosophy of mind can make progress.

3. Transparent computationalism: computation without GOFAI dogmatism

Chrisley’s position on computation is subtle. In “Transparent Computationalism,” Chrisley distinguishes between an opaque and a transparent reading of the claim that cognition is computation. The opaque reading identifies computation with whatever our current formal theories say computation is: algorithms, Turing machines, complexity theory, or symbolic manipulation. The transparent reading instead treats computation as a real phenomenon that our theories are trying to capture, and keeps open the possibility that our current formal theories may be incomplete.

This distinction lets Chrisley keep the central computationalist insight while rejecting narrow computational dogma. If cognition is computational, it need not follow that cognition is algorithmic in a familiar symbolic sense. It also need not follow that disembodied formal manipulation is enough for mind. A transparent computationalist can say: present theories of computation may be too narrow for mentality, but the right account of mentality may still be computational once computation itself is better understood.

This matters for artificial consciousness. Many anti-AI arguments attack a specific picture: a disembodied symbol manipulator executing formal rules. Chrisley’s transparent computationalism avoids that target. It allows computation to be embodied, embedded, dynamical, sub-symbolic, interactive, and architecture-dependent. It is therefore neither old-fashioned symbolic AI nor anti-computational enactivism.

This is one of Chrisley’s most historically important moves. Chrisley belongs to the post-GOFAI generation, but does not abandon computation in favor of vague organismic or purely biological language. Instead, Chrisley asks what computation must become if it is to explain embodied intelligence and consciousness. That makes Chrisley a bridge figure between computational functionalism and embodied cognitive science.

4. Embodiment and nonconceptual content

Chrisley’s early work on embodiment focuses on content, especially nonconceptual content. A mental state’s content, for Chrisley, is not merely the information it carries or the proposition it makes true or false. Content is the way the world is presented to a subject. This makes content both normative and perspectival: it can present things correctly or incorrectly, and it can present them in a way tied to the subject’s capacities for perception and action.

Nonconceptual content is content that does not require the subject to possess fully articulated concepts. It is especially important in perception, motor control, infancy, animal cognition, and embodied interaction. Chrisley’s point is not simply that non-human animals or infants have less sophisticated beliefs. The stronger point is that some forms of content are structured by sensorimotor capacities rather than by linguistic or propositional concepts.

This produces a problem for philosophy of mind: ordinary linguistic specification, such as “the subject represents that p,” is often inadequate. A “that”-clause imposes a conceptual structure that the content itself may not have. If the content is spatial, motoric, action-oriented, indexical, pre-objective, or dynamically tied to bodily capacities, then a sentence may misrepresent it.

Chrisley’s solution is not to abandon representation, but to look for new ways to specify content. Some content may require reference to the subject’s embodiment, environmental embedding, perceptual capacities, motor possibilities, and actual temporal interaction. In some cases, the best specification of content may require an artificial or robotic system that instantiates the relevant capacities. This is why robotics becomes philosophically important: it can provide active, manipulable, embodied formats for specifying forms of content that language alone cannot adequately capture.

This is the basis of Chrisley’s later synthetic phenomenology. The line from early embodiment to artificial consciousness is direct: if phenomenal content includes nonconceptual, embodied, sensorimotor structure, then artifacts may be needed not only to reproduce consciousness but to specify what conscious experience is like.

5. Synthetic phenomenology: artificial systems as specifications of experience

Chrisley’s synthetic phenomenology is one of the most original parts of the program. It asks how artificial systems can help characterize phenomenal states. It has two main forms. In one form, an artifact is used to characterize phenomenal states possessed or modeled by that artifact. In the other, an artifact is used to specify phenomenal states whether or not those states belong to the artifact itself. This distinction is crucial: a machine need not be conscious in order to contribute to the science of consciousness.

The underlying problem is that consciousness science needs a way to specify its explananda. If we want to explain visual experience, pain, spatial presence, or “what it is like,” we need a way to say precisely which experiential content is being explained. Linguistic descriptions may be too coarse, too conceptual, or too detached from sensorimotor organization. Synthetic phenomenology proposes that artifacts can help specify the phenomenal contents under investigation.

Chrisley distinguishes evocative, referential, and mixed modes of content specification. An evocative specification tries to get the audience to undergo something relevant. A referential specification tries to pick out the content by discriminating it. A mixed specification combines these. Chrisley’s preferred examples involve enactive depictions, in which an artifact presents not just a static image but a structure of expectations, actions, and possible changes.

The SEER-3 example illustrates this. A robotic system with foveated visual processing and action-dependent expectations can generate depictions of the content of its visual state: not simply “what it sees” as a camera image, but what it expects would be available under possible movements or perceptual actions. In Chrisley’s discussion, the robot’s current input plus its structured expectations determine a kind of visual content; the artifact can then help specify that content for theorists.

This is not an argument that SEER-3 is conscious. Chrisley is careful: synthetic phenomenology can be discriminative rather than constitutive. It may help us discriminate or specify phenomenal content without claiming that the artifact itself has phenomenal consciousness. This is a recurring Chrisley strategy: avoid premature claims of full artificial consciousness while showing that artificial systems can play indispensable roles in consciousness research.

Synthetic phenomenology also shows why Chrisley is not satisfied with theories that define consciousness purely abstractly. The phenomenal target may be partly grasped through interaction with artifacts. A theory of consciousness that cannot tell us how to specify the relevant experiential contents has not yet fully connected to its explanandum.

6. Artificial consciousness: not one thesis, but a taxonomy

Chrisley’s 2008 “Philosophical Foundations of Artificial Consciousness” is best read as a conceptual map of the field. Chrisley argues that pessimism about AI and consciousness is often based on crude distinctions. The debate is usually framed as: either artificial systems really are conscious in the strong sense, or they are merely simulations and therefore philosophically uninteresting. Chrisley rejects that dichotomy.

A central concept here is lagom artificial consciousness. “Lagom” means something like “just enough” or “appropriately moderate.” In Chrisley’s usage, lagom artificial consciousness avoids both maximal strong-AI claims and trivial weak-AI claims. A lagom artificial-consciousness project might identify necessary conditions for consciousness, build tools that help specify consciousness, provide discriminative tests, or generate architectures that are practically required for progress, without claiming to have produced a fully conscious machine.

Chrisley also distinguishes scientific from engineering aims, autonomous from prosthetic artificial consciousness, and several roles artificial systems may play: constitutive, discriminative, practically necessary, conceptually necessary, and prosthetic. This matters because one artifact might fail to be conscious and still be philosophically important. For example, a synthetic-phenomenology system may not have experience but may help specify experiential content. A robot may not instantiate consciousness but may reveal architectural requirements for it.

This taxonomy is philosophically powerful because it undermines an unhelpful all-or-nothing debate. The question is not only “Can we build a conscious machine?” It is also “Can artificial systems help us specify, discriminate, model, explain, or scaffold consciousness?” Chrisley’s answer is yes, and that answer is independent of whether any current system is conscious.

The taxonomy also clarifies the historical role of artificial consciousness. For Chrisley, artificial consciousness is not merely the final engineering goal of making conscious robots. It is a methodological framework for doing philosophy of mind and consciousness science. That is why artificial consciousness has importance even before strong artificial consciousness is achieved.

7. Virtual-machine functionalism: the architecture of mind

Chrisley’s work with Aaron Sloman develops virtual-machine functionalism. The idea is that mental life is best understood not as a set of simple input-output dispositions, but as a structured virtual-machine architecture containing multiple coexisting, interacting processes: perceptions, goals, memories, deliberations, affective states, self-monitoring states, and metacognitive processes. Mental components are not physical parts in a simple anatomical sense; they are virtual-machine components realized by physical systems.

This is a significant revision of standard functionalism. Standard functionalism is often criticized for being too behavioristic, too coarse, or too dependent on simple causal-role descriptions. Virtual-machine functionalism allows internal complexity: concurrently active processes, hidden internal states, meta-management layers, and architecture-dependent forms of mentality. It is therefore more suitable for explaining consciousness than a simple mapping from inputs to outputs.

Chrisley and Sloman treat consciousness as a cluster of aspects of human and animal information processing. This does not mean consciousness is unreal; it means that ordinary consciousness concepts may be indeterminate and should be replaced or refined by more exact architecture-based concepts. Instead of asking whether a system has “consciousness” in one unanalyzed sense, we ask which architectural features it has: perceptual access, deliberation, affect, self-monitoring, meta-management, internal attention, and so on.

The meta-management layer is especially important. Human-like consciousness may require mechanisms that monitor, evaluate, and partly control deliberative processes, as well as mechanisms that access intermediate perceptual and action-related states. This enables a system not merely to process the world, but to process aspects of its own processing.

This architecture-based approach also changes philosophy’s task. The aim is not merely to analyze ordinary mental vocabulary. It is to develop successor concepts tied to architectural kinds. In this respect, Chrisley’s philosophy resembles scientific revision: ordinary terms such as “experience,” “qualia,” or “awareness” are not simply preserved or eliminated; they are reconstructed in more precise architectural terms.

8. Qualia: revisionary realism, not eliminativism

Chrisley’s position on qualia is one of the most delicate parts of the view. The traditional concept of qualia often involves four features: intrinsicness, ineffability, privacy, and immediacy. Eliminativists argue that nothing has all these features, so qualia do not exist. Chrisley and Sloman reject that inference. They agree that qualia may not have the properties philosophers traditionally attribute to them, but they do not conclude that qualia are simply nonexistent.

The position is revisionist. Qualia may be real, but not as they seem. Chrisley and Sloman compare this with illusion rather than hallucination: there is something there, but it is mischaracterized. The target of explanation is not a spooky private object but the real architecture-based features that make agents think, say, and reason as if their experiences were intrinsic, ineffable, private, and immediate.

They describe this as a kind of ontologically conservative heterophenomenology. Dennettian heterophenomenology often explains reports of experience without committing to qualia as traditionally understood. Chrisley and Sloman accept the need to explain qualia-talk and qualia-belief, but they are more conservative ontologically: they leave room for the existence of real experience-features that people are trying, imperfectly, to describe.

In virtual-machine terms, qualia are associated with internal states or components to which an agent has special forms of access. A system may monitor intermediate perceptual data, attend to its own internal processes, and form beliefs about those processes. Those beliefs may generate the impression that the relevant features are private, ineffable, intrinsic, and immediate, even if a mature theory explains them in architectural and computational terms.

This avoids both naive qualia realism and eliminativism. Against naive realism, Chrisley denies that traditional qualia properties must be accepted at face value. Against eliminativism, Chrisley denies that rejecting those properties proves there are no qualia-relevant phenomena. The correct target is the architecture that makes qualia seem the way they seem.

A striking implication is that artificial agents might have qualia in this revised sense. If a robot has the right virtual-machine architecture, internal self-monitoring, metacognitive beliefs, and access to intermediate perceptual states, then it might possess the relevant architecture-based features. Chrisley and Sloman even allow the provocative possibility that artificial agents could have unified qualia-relevant structures even if humans turn out not to have exactly the unified structures philosophers have imagined.

9. The Knowledge Argument and meta-knowledge

Chrisley’s 2020 work on artificial consciousness and meta-knowledge directly addresses Jackson’s Knowledge Argument. The standard Knowledge Argument imagines Mary, who knows all physical facts about color but has never seen red; when she sees red, she seems to learn something new, suggesting that physical knowledge was incomplete. Chrisley argues that the fully physically omniscient Mary scenario is logically inconsistent once meta-knowledge is taken seriously.

The core idea is that there are physical facts about Mary’s own epistemic situation that Mary cannot know before seeing red, if she has never seen red. In particular, there are facts involving what she would know, fail to know, or be in a position to know under conditions of experience. If “all physical facts” includes these recursive or self-referential epistemic facts, then Mary cannot both know all physical facts and lack the relevant experience.

This is not a minor technical objection. It fits Chrisley’s broader architecture-based view: knowing what an experience is like is partly a matter of possessing the right meta-knowledge, and acquiring an experience can transform the agent’s self-referential epistemic state. The new thing Mary learns need not be a non-physical fact; it may be a physical or computational meta-fact about her own experiential and epistemic architecture.

For artificial consciousness, the implication is important. If phenomenal knowledge involves meta-knowledge, then a machine-consciousness architecture must support not only perception or behavior, but also forms of self-referential knowledge about its own cognitive and experiential states. In symbolic architectures, one can formally add knowledge predicates; in grounded or sub-symbolic architectures, building genuine meta-knowledge is harder. Chrisley treats this as an architectural challenge rather than an impossibility proof.

This also clarifies why current large AI systems should not automatically be treated as conscious. Linguistic fluency alone is not enough. The issue is whether the system has the relevant grounded, self-referential, metacognitive, architecture-based relations to its own states. Chrisley’s view does not license a simple “it talks about qualia, therefore it has qualia” inference.

10. Grounded metacognitive architectures

Chrisley’s later work emphasizes metacognition: cognition about cognition. Several theories of consciousness already give metacognition an important role, especially higher-order theories. Chrisley’s specific contribution is to ask how metacognition could be implemented in grounded, sub-symbolic, embodied architectures rather than only in symbolic systems.

This matters because grounded architectures are often good at sensorimotor interaction but poor at explicit abstraction. They can learn embodied patterns tied to the here-and-now, but it is not obvious how they can represent their own representational states. Chrisley sees this as a central obstacle for machine consciousness: not just how to build intelligent action, but how to build grounded self-access, self-modeling, and metacognitive expectation.

One proposal is that sensory phenomenology may be linked to sensorimotor expectations, while cognitive phenomenology may be linked to metacognitive expectations. That is, what it is like to see may partly involve expectations about possible sensory change under action, while what it is like to think may involve expectations about one’s own cognitive processes. This is not presented as a complete final theory, but it shows how Chrisley tries to integrate embodied enactivist themes with virtual-machine metacognition.

In “Architectural Requirements for Consciousness,” Chrisley and Sloman sketch requirements for a system that can have beliefs about its own states, infer relations among those beliefs, and treat some internal states as reasons or justifications. Their aim is not to over-intellectualize consciousness; they explicitly want the account to remain compatible with animals and infants having qualia. But for human-like reflective consciousness, metacognitive architecture becomes central.

The result is a layered picture. Basic consciousness-like phenomena may involve embodied sensorimotor contents. More sophisticated subjective consciousness involves internal access to those contents. Human-like qualia-talk and philosophical puzzlement require metacognitive beliefs about those internal states. Artificial consciousness, therefore, is not a single switch; it is a family of increasingly structured architectural capacities.

11. Chrisley’s current stance on conscious AI

Chrisley’s recent stance remains moderate. In a 2025 talk description, Chrisley rejects both extremes: that AI consciousness is impossible and that present AI models already have consciousness or will automatically have it through scaling. That is exactly what one would expect from the earlier taxonomy of artificial consciousness: Chrisley does not reduce the issue to a hype cycle or a simple impossibility theorem.

In a 2026 accepted Behavioral and Brain Sciences commentary, Chrisley argues that conscious AI does not require computational functionalism, and that consciousness may be non-biological in the way flight is non-biological. The flight analogy is important. Biological birds fly using biological mechanisms, but flight itself is not restricted to those mechanisms. Similarly, even if human consciousness depends on biological features, it would not follow that non-biological artificial consciousness is impossible.

This is an important refinement. Chrisley is not merely saying, “functionalism is true, therefore AI consciousness is possible.” The 2026 argument is stronger and more flexible: even if strict computational functionalism fails, that does not by itself establish biological exclusivity. This makes Chrisley’s view resilient against some anti-functionalist arguments.

Thus the mature position is: current AI consciousness is not established; scale and verbal performance are not enough; biological exclusivity is not warranted; artificial systems may be conscious if they have the right architecture; and artificial systems can help consciousness research even before they are conscious.

12. Reconstructed comprehensive philosophy of mind

From the corpus, we can extract a systematic philosophy of mind with several theses.

First, mind is architecture-realized rather than substance-based. Mental states are not ghostly inner objects, and they are not merely behavior. They are components, processes, and capacities within complex architectures realized by physical systems. The relevant architecture may be computational in a transparent sense, virtual-machine-based, embodied, and dynamically embedded in the environment.

Second, ordinary mental concepts are often too coarse. Terms such as consciousness, experience, qualia, attention, and awareness are not useless, but they are not final scientific categories. Philosophy of mind should help produce successor concepts tied to architectures, functions, and forms of interaction. This is why Chrisley treats consciousness as a cluster phenomenon rather than a single unanalyzed essence.

Third, content is presentational and often nonconceptual. Mental content is the way the world is presented to a subject, not merely a proposition encoded in the head. Some contents are structured by embodied capacities for perception and action rather than by language-like concepts. This makes embodiment philosophically indispensable.

Fourth, phenomenal content may require synthetic specification. Because nonconceptual and sensorimotor contents may not be adequately expressed in language, artificial systems can help specify them. Synthetic phenomenology is therefore a method for consciousness science: it uses artifacts to characterize experiential contents, whether or not the artifacts themselves are conscious.

Fifth, qualia are real targets for explanation, but not as traditionally conceived. Qualia need not be intrinsically private, ineffable, immediate, and non-physical. The proper explanandum is the architecture-based phenomenon that gives rise to qualia-beliefs and qualia-talk, and perhaps also to real experience-features misdescribed by traditional philosophy. This is revisionary realism or ontologically conservative heterophenomenology.

Sixth, metacognition is central to human-like consciousness. A system that merely processes stimuli may have some important capacities, but human-like subjective consciousness involves internal self-access, self-monitoring, and beliefs or proto-beliefs about one’s own internal states. This is why meta-management, meta-knowledge, and grounded metacognitive architecture become central in Chrisley’s later work.

Seventh, philosophical knowledge of consciousness is partly practical and interactive. Consciousness concepts are not mastered only by knowing definitions. They may require experiential and engineering-mediated skills. Philosophers may need to interact with artificial systems in order to acquire better concepts. This is the methodological core of interactive empiricism.

Eighth, artificial consciousness is plural. It includes not only the creation of conscious machines, but also artificial systems that model, discriminate, specify, scaffold, or clarify consciousness. The strong/artificial-consciousness question remains important, but it is only one part of a larger research program.

Ninth, conscious AI is possible but not cheap. Chrisley does not infer consciousness from impressive outputs, linguistic fluency, or scaling. The relevant question is architectural: whether a system has the right grounded content, virtual-machine organization, self-access, metacognition, and possibly synthetic-phenomenological capacities. Current AI models are not thereby conscious merely because they are fluent.

Taken together, these theses form a philosophy of mind that could be named embodied transparent-computational virtual-machine revisionism. Less technically: Chrisley’s view is a philosophy of mind in which minds are embodied computational architectures, consciousness is an architecture-sensitive cluster of capacities, qualia are real but misdescribed architecture-based phenomena, and artificial systems are both possible conscious subjects and indispensable tools for understanding consciousness.

13. Comparison with major positions in philosophy of mind

Compared with classical functionalism, Chrisley is more architecturally detailed and less behavioristic. Mental states are not merely input-output roles; they are situated within multilayer virtual machines with internal processes, self-monitoring, and meta-management. This gives Chrisley a richer functionalism than the textbook version.

Compared with old computationalism, Chrisley is less formalist and less symbolic. Transparent computationalism refuses to identify cognition with currently dominant formal models of computation. This lets Chrisley accept much of the embodied critique of GOFAI without giving up computational explanation.

Compared with enactivism and embodied cognition, Chrisley is more computational and representational. Chrisley accepts that perception and content are deeply tied to action, embodiment, and environmental embedding, but does not conclude that representation or computation should be abandoned. Instead, these notions must be reconstructed around embodied systems.

Compared with Dennett, Chrisley is less eliminativist about qualia. Chrisley shares the heterophenomenological impulse to explain qualia-talk and qualia-belief, but does not treat the failure of traditional qualia properties as proof that qualia do not exist. The position is revisionary rather than eliminative.

Compared with Chalmers-style hard-problem philosophy, Chrisley gives more weight to conceptual change, architecture, and artificial-system construction. Chrisley does not simply solve the hard problem by stipulation. Rather, the strategy is to show how some of the pressure behind the hard problem may come from unstable concepts of qualia, poor methods of specifying experience, and inadequate architectural models of self-access.

Compared with biological naturalism or anti-AI exclusivism, Chrisley is more open to artificial consciousness. Even if biological systems realize consciousness in ways that current computational functionalism does not capture, that does not establish that non-biological consciousness is impossible. The 2026 flight analogy makes this point explicit.

Compared with current AI-consciousness hype, Chrisley is more demanding. The fact that a system produces fluent language about consciousness is not enough. The serious question is whether it has the right grounded architecture, meta-knowledge, self-access, and relation to its own internal states.

14. What Chrisley contributes to the philosophy of artificial consciousness

Chrisley’s first major contribution is conceptual pluralism. Artificial consciousness is not a single yes-or-no project. It includes strong, weak, lagom, autonomous, prosthetic, discriminative, and engineering roles. This prevents the field from collapsing into premature declarations that machines are conscious or premature dismissals that artificial consciousness is impossible.

The second contribution is methodological constructivism. Chrisley argues that building and interacting with systems can change the concepts with which we theorize consciousness. This is stronger than saying that engineering supplies data. It says engineering can be part of philosophical concept formation.

The third contribution is synthetic phenomenology. Artificial systems can help specify experiential contents, especially nonconceptual and sensorimotor contents that are poorly captured by language. This gives machine-consciousness research a role even when the machines involved are not themselves conscious.

The fourth contribution is architecture-based qualia theory. Instead of asking whether qualia are non-physical intrinsic simples, Chrisley asks what kinds of architecture produce the beliefs and self-relations associated with qualia. This transforms the qualia debate from metaphysical stalemate into a design and explanation problem.

The fifth contribution is metacognitive artificial-consciousness design. Chrisley’s later work shows why a serious machine-consciousness architecture must go beyond task performance and perception. It must address self-knowledge, meta-knowledge, internal monitoring, and grounded cognition about cognition.

15. Open problems and limitations

The first limitation is that Chrisley does not give a final necessary-and-sufficient theory of consciousness. This is not accidental: the whole program resists premature closure. But critics may reasonably ask whether the result is too programmatic. We get architectural directions, taxonomies, and methodological tools rather than a completed criterion for consciousness.

The second limitation is that revisionary qualia theory may be accused of explaining qualia-talk rather than phenomenality itself. Chrisley’s answer is that the real phenomena behind qualia-talk may be architecture-based and misdescribed, not nonexistent. Still, a hard-problem realist may argue that the explanatory target has shifted from experience to reports and beliefs about experience.

The third limitation concerns validation. Interactive empiricism says that philosophical concepts may improve through interaction with artificial systems, but it is difficult to specify when such concept change is truth-tracking rather than merely persuasive, immersive, or technologically seductive. Chrisley explicitly treats the process as rational and reality-tracking, but the standards for success remain a difficult methodological issue.

The fourth limitation concerns metacognition. If metacognition is central, the theory may risk over-intellectualizing consciousness and excluding infants or animals. Chrisley and Sloman explicitly try to avoid that result by distinguishing sophisticated beliefs about qualia from more basic conscious phenomena, but the exact boundary remains open.

The fifth limitation concerns ethics. Chrisley’s architecture-based approach helps ask whether artificial systems could be conscious, but it does not by itself settle when artificial systems deserve moral consideration. If artificial qualia are possible, then future systems might become moral patients; however, the bridge from architecture to welfare, suffering, or rights requires further argument.

These limitations are real, but they are also part of the program’s honesty. Chrisley’s view is not a simple consciousness test. It is a disciplined framework for turning vague metaphysical disputes into architecture, content-specification, and metacognitive design questions.

16. Final synthesis

Chrisley’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows: minds are embodied, environmentally embedded, computationally organized virtual-machine architectures. Consciousness is a cluster of architecture-dependent phenomena involving sensorimotor content, internal access, metacognition, and evolving concepts of experience. Qualia are not the mysterious intrinsic objects of traditional philosophy, but neither are they nothing; they are real or potentially real architecture-based phenomena that generate and partly justify qualia-talk while being misdescribed by traditional theory. Artificial systems matter because they may instantiate consciousness, but also because they can help specify, model, discriminate, and transform our concepts of consciousness.

Historically, Chrisley’s position is a bridge between functionalism and embodied cognition, between computationalism and enactivism, between Dennettian heterophenomenology and qualia realism, and between classical AI debates and contemporary AI-consciousness research. Its deepest philosophical claim is methodological: consciousness may not be understood by passive analysis alone. To understand it, we may need to build, interact, revise, and build again.

PreviousOwen Holland NextMurray Shanahan

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
